instruments: Act on Investment Trusts and Investment Corporations (投資信託及び投資法人に関する法律, Act No. 198 of 4 June 1951) -- Art. 2(1) defines a settlor-directed investment trust (委託者指図型投資信託) as a trust operated as investment primarily in securities, real estate, and other assets designated by Cabinet Order as necessary to facilitate investment -- termed '特定資産' (specified assets) -- confirmed by direct fetch of the current consolidated text via the e-Gov Law API, law_id 326AC0100000198

Order for Enforcement of the Act on Investment Trusts and Investment Corporations (投資信託及び投資法人に関する法律施行令, Cabinet Order No. 480 of 2000), Art. 3 (特定資産の範囲, 'Scope of Specified Assets') -- enumerates twelve specified-asset categories: (1) securities; (2) rights pertaining to derivative transactions, with an express parenthetical carve-out excluding anything relating to cryptoassets (暗号等資産, cross-referencing Financial Instruments and Exchange Act Art. 2(24)(iii-2)) or to cryptoasset-related financial indicators (暗号等資産関連金融指標, cross-referencing FIEA Art. 185-22(1)(i)); (3) real estate; (4) real-estate lease rights; (5) surface rights; (6) promissory notes; (7) monetary claims; (8) anonymous-partnership investment interests; (9) commodities; (10) commodity-investment-transaction rights; (11) renewable-energy power-generation equipment; (12) public-facility operation rights -- cryptoassets appear in none of the twelve items, and Item 2's own text affirmatively excludes crypto-related derivatives -- confirmed by direct fetch of the current, live consolidated text via the e-Gov Law API, law_id 412CO0000000480

FSA, 'Examination of the Regulatory Systems Related to Cryptoassets' (Discussion Paper, 10 April 2025, FSA's own Provisional Translation), footnote 21 -- the FSA's own English-language statement of the specified-assets bar and its consequence for domestic crypto ETFs

Financial System Council, Working Group on the Crypto-Asset System, Report (金融審議会 暗号資産制度に関するワーキング・グループ報告, 10 December 2025) -- Japanese only, no official English translation located in this research; frames the policy backdrop (investment-grade adoption of cryptoassets domestically and abroad, including foreign spot Bitcoin ETFs) and recommends migrating crypto-asset business regulation from the Payment Services Act (PSA) to the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (FIEA), tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar

FSA, FY2026 (Reiwa 8) tax-reform request, '暗号資産取引に係る課税の見直し' ('Review of Taxation Relating to Cryptoasset Transactions'), item 03-1/03-2/03-3, submitted in the government's annual tax-request cycle -- FSA's own primary statement that enabling domestic crypto-ETF formation, including on the tax side, is under consideration, and its citation of the Cabinet's 13 June 2025 'Grand Design and Implementation Plan for a New Form of Capitalism' as the originating policy direction

FSA, '令和8（2026）年度税制改正について -- 税制改正大綱における金融庁関係の主要項目 --' (FSA's own summary of the FY2026 tax-reform outline, published December 2025), pages 4-5 -- FSA's own statement that crypto-asset ETF taxation measures are adopted on the premise of amendment of both the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Investment Trust Act's enforcement order, with a summary table showing the current 'cannot be formed' status and the planned sequencing to formation via ordinance amendment plus 20% separate self-assessed taxation

Bill for Partial Amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act (金融商品取引法及び資金決済に関する法律の一部を改正する法律案) -- submitted to the 221st ordinary Diet session 10 April 2026; passed the House of Representatives 11 June 2026; pending in the House of Councillors' Committee on Financial Affairs as of this card's generation date, per direct fetch of the House of Councillors' own bill-status page -- the FIEA/PSA migration bill that FSA's own tax-outline materials name as the precondition for the Investment Trust Act enforcement-order amendment; tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar
standing_summary: Japan has no domestic crypto-asset exchange-traded fund and no crypto-asset investment trust today, and the reason is a specific, directly verifiable statutory gap rather than an unwritten policy choice -- consistent with the 'amend and apply existing law' pattern that runs through every other pillar of this jurisdiction. Investment trusts are governed by the Act on Investment Trusts and Investment Corporations (投資信託及び投資法人に関する法律, Act No. 198 of 4 June 1951); this research fetched its current consolidated text directly via the e-Gov Law API and confirmed that Art. 2(1) defines a settlor-directed investment trust as a trust operated as investment primarily in securities, real estate, and other assets 'designated by Cabinet Order as necessary to facilitate investment' -- termed 特定資産 (specified assets) -- with the exact scope delegated entirely to Cabinet Order. That Cabinet Order, the Order for Enforcement of the Act on Investment Trusts and Investment Corporations (投資信託及び投資法人に関する法律施行令, Cabinet Order No. 480 of 2000), sets out the closed list at Art. 3 (特定資産の範囲); this research fetched its current, live consolidated text directly via the same API and found twelve enumerated categories -- securities; rights pertaining to derivative transactions; real estate; real-estate lease rights; surface rights; promissory notes; monetary claims; anonymous-partnership investment interests; commodities; commodity-investment-transaction rights; renewable-energy power-generation equipment; and public-facility operation rights. Cryptoassets appear in none of the twelve items. The bar is in fact doubled: Item 2's own parenthetical text goes out of its way to exclude, from 'rights pertaining to derivative transactions,' anything relating to 暗号等資産 (cryptoassets, defined by cross-reference to Financial Instruments and Exchange Act Art. 2(24)(iii-2)) or to 暗号等資産関連金融指標 (cryptoasset-related financial indicators, defined by cross-reference to FIEA Art. 185-22(1)(i)) -- so cryptoassets held directly have no category to sit in, and cryptoasset derivatives are affirmatively carved out of the one category that might otherwise have reached them. FSA's own April 2025 discussion paper states the resulting consequence directly, in FSA's own English-language provisional translation, at footnote 21: 'In Japan, under current laws, cryptoassets are not classified as "specified assets" that may serve as the primary investment target for investment trusts, and therefore ETFs targeting cryptoassets cannot be constituted (see Article 3 of the Order for Enforcement of the Act on Investment Trusts and Investment Corporations).' The Financial System Council's Working Group on the Crypto-Asset System, in its report of 10 December 2025 (Japanese only; no official English translation located during this research), does not itself restate that Art. 3 bar, but frames the surrounding policy backdrop: it records that, as of end-October 2025, registered exchange service providers held more than 13 million cumulative user accounts and over JPY 5 trillion in user-deposited assets, and it cites the international spread of spot Bitcoin ETFs in the United States, Canada and Australia -- including participation by long-horizon institutional investors -- as part of the WG's stated case for treating fair price formation and trading of cryptoassets as an increasingly pressing concern; the WG's core structural recommendation, migrating crypto-asset business regulation from the Payment Services Act into the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, is tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar of this jurisdiction and not restated here. The concrete, officially stated path from that FIEA migration toward an eventual domestic crypto ETF runs through Japan's annual tax-reform process. On 13 June 2025 the Cabinet decided the 'Grand Design and Implementation Plan for a New Form of Capitalism,' directing that, alongside early Diet submission of investor-protection legislation, the government would also consider a review of the tax system 'including introduction of separate taxation' for cryptoassets -- quoted here via FSA's own citation of that Cabinet Decision in its FY2026 tax-reform request, not independently re-fetched from the Cabinet Decision text itself in this research pass. Acting on that direction, FSA's own FY2026 tax-reform request states plainly, in FSA's own words: '併せて、諸外国の動向を踏まえ、我が国でも暗号資産ETFの組成を可能とするための検討を税制面を含めて行う必要' (Japanese-original; the request states that, in addition to separate taxation generally, consideration -- including on the tax-system side -- is needed to enable the formation of cryptoasset ETFs in Japan, in light of trends in other countries). FSA's own December 2025 summary of the resulting ruling-party tax-reform outline is the most precise official statement located in this research: it states that the outline's crypto-taxation measures are adopted '金融商品取引法等の改正を前提に' (Japanese-original; premised on amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, among other laws) and, specifically for ETFs, that '暗号資産ETF等については、投信法施行令の改正を前提に、分離課税とする' (Japanese-original; crypto-asset ETFs, etc. are to be made subject to separate taxation, premised on amendment of the Investment Trust Act's enforcement order) -- naming, in substance, the exact Cabinet Order provision (Art. 3 of Order No. 480/2000) this research independently fetched and confirmed above, and tying its amendment explicitly to enactment of the pending FIEA bill. The same FSA document's own summary table states the sequencing for an ETF investing in 'certain cryptoassets' (一定の暗号資産, there defined as cryptoassets handled by a registered cryptoasset-transaction business operator) even more starkly: '現在は組成不可（政令改正必要）⇒政令改正により組成可能とする⇒申告分離課税 20%' (Japanese-original; currently cannot be formed [ordinance amendment required] -- to be made formable via ordinance amendment -- 20% separate self-assessed taxation), the same 20% rate (split 15% national income tax plus 5% resident tax under the outline) that the table applies to listed-share and existing-ETF capital gains today, replacing the aggregate-income treatment -- up to 55% -- that applies to cryptoasset gains under current law. The FIEA/PSA bill that both the WG report and the tax outline treat as the ETF pathway's precondition -- the Bill for Partial Amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act -- was submitted to the 221st ordinary Diet session on 10 April 2026 and passed the House of Representatives on 11 June 2026; this research directly fetched the House of Councillors' own bill-status page, which as of this card's generation date showed the bill referred to the Councillors' Committee on Financial Affairs with both the committee-vote and plenary-vote date fields blank -- i.e., not yet enacted. Contemporaneous press reporting, not independently verified against any primary Diet or FSA source in this research pass and therefore not asserted as fact, describes a committee vote expected around 14 July 2026 and floor passage expected by the ordinary session's scheduled close around 17 July 2026; this pillar's next revision should re-confirm actual passage and promulgation directly against the House of Councillors' bill page or FSA's own Diet-submissions listing before treating enactment as settled. Until both the FIEA bill and the Investment Trust Act enforcement-order amendment it is officially stated to precede are actually enacted, the position FSA's own April 2025 discussion paper described remains the operative law: cryptoassets cannot be the primary investment target of a Japanese investment trust, and no domestic crypto-asset ETF can be constituted. This pillar tracks only investment-trust and ETF product eligibility; the FIEA/PSA migration bill's business-registration, information-disclosure and insider-trading provisions are tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar, security tokens (electronically recorded transferable rights under FIEA) are tracked in the Tokenization & RWA pillar as a distinct FIEA-securities category with no bearing on the specified-assets analysis described here, and crypto-asset investment-management and advisory business regulation generally is tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar -- none of that content is duplicated in this card.

open_items: The specific Cabinet Order text that would amend Art. 3 of Order No. 480/2000 to add cryptoassets as a specified-asset category has not itself been drafted or published for public comment as of this research pass; FSA's own December 2025 tax-reform-outline document states only that ETF formation is 'premised on' such an amendment, without further detail. This card does not assume any particular drafting approach (e.g., a dedicated crypto-ETF category versus a general amendment to Item 2's derivatives carve-out) beyond what FSA's own document states.

Contemporaneous secondary press (e.g. Nikkei, CoinDesk, and other outlets) reports a target year of 2028 for the Cabinet Order amendment and resulting domestic crypto-ETF listings, and separately reports that several Japanese securities and asset-management firms are exploring future crypto investment-trust or ETF products. No primary FSA or Financial System Council document naming a specific target year, or naming any specific firm in this connection, was located during this research pass. Consistent with this jurisdiction's named-entity rule, no firm is named in this card, and the 2028 date is recorded here only as an open item, not as an FSA-stated timeline.

The Bill for Partial Amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act was pending in the House of Councillors' Committee on Financial Affairs as of this card's generation date (2026-07-12), per direct fetch of the House of Councillors' own bill-status page, with both the committee-vote and plenary-vote fields blank. Press reporting anticipating a committee vote around 14 July 2026 and floor passage by around 17 July 2026 is not independently confirmed against a primary source in this pass. This card's status_seal and standing_summary should be re-verified against the House of Councillors' bill page (or FSA's own Diet-submissions listing) at the next research pass, and updated promptly if the bill has since passed and been promulgated.

The Financial System Council WG report of 10 December 2025 was read in full in Japanese during this research pass; it does not itself restate the Art. 3 specified-assets bar or the FIEA-precondition sequencing for ETFs described in this card -- those points are sourced instead from FSA's April 2025 discussion paper (footnote 21) and FSA's own December 2025 tax-reform-outline summary, respectively. No official English translation of the WG report was located; all WG report content in this card is drawn from direct reading of the Japanese-original PDF and is flagged as such.

The 13 June 2025 Cabinet Decision, 'Grand Design and Implementation Plan for a New Form of Capitalism,' is quoted in this card only via FSA's own citation of it within FSA's FY2026 tax-reform request; this research did not independently fetch and verify the Cabinet Decision's own primary text (e.g., from the Cabinet Secretariat's website) in this pass.

Boundary note: this pillar addresses only investment-trust and ETF product eligibility for cryptoassets under the Act on Investment Trusts and Investment Corporations and its enforcement order. The pending FIEA/PSA migration bill's business-registration, information-disclosure and insider-trading provisions are tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar; security tokens (electronically recorded transferable rights, ERTRs) under FIEA are tracked in the Tokenization & RWA pillar as a distinct category with no bearing on the specified-assets analysis here; and crypto-asset investment-management and advisory business regulation generally is tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar. None of that content is duplicated in this card.
